elements.sent-2411-001: The paper describes a system designed by Advacheck team to recognise machine-generated and human-written texts in the monolingual subtask of GenAI Detection Task 1 competition.
elements.sent-2411-002: Our developed system is a multi-task architecture with shared Transformer Encoder between several classification heads.
elements.sent-2411-003: One head is responsible for binary classification between human-written and machine-generated texts, while the other heads are auxiliary multiclass classifiers for texts of different domains from particular datasets.
elements.sent-2411-004: As multiclass heads were trained to distinguish the domains presented in the data, they provide a better understanding of the samples.
elements.sent-2411-005: This approach led us to achieve the first place in the official ranking with 83.07\% macro $F_1$-score on the test set and bypass the baseline by 10\%.
elements.sent-2411-006: We further study obtained system through ablation, error and representation analyses, finding that multi-task learning outperforms single-task mode and simultaneous tasks form a cluster structure in embeddings space.
elements.sent-2411-008: With the continuous improvement of Large Language Models (LLMs), the task of detection machine-generated texts demands more and more attention from the community.
elements.sent-2411-009: The potential cases of misuse include malicious usage by students~\cite{zeng2023automatic, koike2023outfox and scientists~\cite{ma2023ai, aij23 .}}
elements.sent-2411-010: Furthermore, this is often the cause of plagiarism~\cite{plag and spam~\cite{spam .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: The mentioned things are encouraging researchers to improve methods for detecting artificial text simultaneously with enhancing generation methods.
elements.sent-2411-012: The task of detection of the machine-generated texts is usually formulated as a binary text classification task~\cite{jawahar-etal-2020-automatic .}
elements.sent-2411-013: The most common solutions are to fine-tune the Transformer-based model~\cite{iber or to use zero-shot approaches with intrinsic statistics of the text~\cite{DetectGPT, zeroshot .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: While these methods perform well on in-domain tasks~\cite{TURINGBENCH , they are not robust to change of the domain, generator model, or language of the texts~\cite{wang2023m4, Tulchinskii_phd, kuznetsov2024robustaigeneratedtextdetection .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Meanwhile, for the detection of AI-content in the wild such a change is, on the contrary, a more realistic setup~\cite{raid .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Beyond this, the data presented for the detection task may be of poor quality, which also complicates the challenge of the detection task~\cite{dataquality .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Therefore, the goal is to obtain a model that is robust to the presence of data of poor quality and with a lot of noise, and, in addition, to make this model capable to adapt to new domains.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.4\textwidth]{images/pipeline.png} \caption{Overview of the proposed multi-task architecture. Modules marked only with \raisebox{-0.25em}{\includegraphics[scale=0.015]{images/fire.pdf}} are trainable at all stages. The weights of Transformer Encoder are frozen \raisebox{-0.25em}{\includegraphics[scale=0.015]{images/snow.pdf}} at the first stage of training and trainable \raisebox{-0.25em}{\includegraphics[scale=0.015]{images/fire.pdf}} at the second one. The Custom Classification Head (CCH) described in Appendix \ref{sec:appendix_a} is used for predictions.} \label{fig:pipeline}
elements.sent-2411-018: The high coherence and quality of writing achieved by modern LLMs makes it difficult to find a specific sample-counted qualitative feature by which it would be possible to create a hyperplane in the space of texts and separate generated from human-generated ones.
elements.sent-2411-019: One of the possible refinements of the representations from the single-task learning architecture is the multi-task learning (MTL)~\cite{mtl, needmore .}
elements.sent-2411-020: It was also noted that systems with MTL architecture had achieved high results in the previous competitions~\cite{mtlsemeval , therefore we decided to utilize this approach in our work.}
elements.sent-2411-021: In this paper we discuss our solution as the Advacheck team at GenAI Content Detection Task 1: English and Multilingual Machine-generated Text Detection: AI vs. Human~\cite{wang2025genai .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Our method shows that with additional internal data analysis and embedding alignment using MTL, it is still possible to achieve high performance in detecting fragments in cross-domain and cross-generator setups on texts from the advanced LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-023: As we forced model to focus on various domains, it allowed us to form a cluster domain-wise structure for the text representations in the vector space.
elements.sent-2411-024: In our research, we show that (1) multi-task learning outperforms the default single-task, (2) cluster structure is formed at the shared encoder (3) compare different configurations of the system and (4) analyse the errors of the system.
elements.sent-2411-025: The monolingual subtask of Task 1: Binary Multilingual Machine-Generated Text Detection focuses on identifying whether the English text was entirely authored by a human or generated by a language model.
elements.sent-2411-026: The competition is the continuation and improvement of the SemEval Shared Task 8 (subtask A)~\cite{semeval2024task8 and combines refreshed training and testing samples from different domains and novel LLMs.}
elements.sent-2411-027: The statistics of the dataset are summarised in Appendix \ref{sec:appendix_c .}
elements.sent-2411-028: The official evaluation metric for the monolingual subtask is Macro $F_1$-score and the additional metric is Micro $F_1$-score.
elements.sent-2411-029: Why multi-task learning? In the current task formulation we have more than 600k texts in the training set with dozens of domains and generation models.
elements.sent-2411-030: Such an amount is very noisy for a binary classification task, because it can be challenging for a model to distinguish between relevant and irrelevant features.
elements.sent-2411-031: Multi-task learning may help the model focus on those features that actually matter as other tasks will update representation of samples with inner information.
elements.sent-2411-032: Our aim is to obtain fine-grained representations of the data that ideally ignores data-dependent noise and generalises well.
elements.sent-2411-033: Since different tasks involve distinct noises, a model trained on multiple tasks simultaneously is able to learn a more general representation.
elements.sent-2411-034: Furthermore, it reduces the risk of overfitting.. Model.
elements.sent-2411-035: We propose a MTL architecture with hard parameter sharing (HPS), it is depicted in Figure \ref{fig:pipeline .}
elements.sent-2411-036: In HPS, a common Transformer-based encoder is used for multiple tasks.
elements.sent-2411-037: After several variations of set of parallel heads, we focused on three custom classification heads (CCH) for simultaneous training:.
elements.sent-2411-038: Binary CCH head for solving the initial monolingual subtask [2 classes].
elements.sent-2411-039: Multiclass CCH to define a sub-source within the HC3~\cite{Su2023HC3PA source [5 classes].}
elements.sent-2411-040: Multiclass CCH for sub-source detection within the M4GT~\cite{wang2023m4 source [6 classes].}
elements.sent-2411-041: The model was trained in two phases: fine-tuning chosen classifiers with frozen \raisebox{-0.25em {\includegraphics[scale=0.015]{images/snow.pdf shared encoder weights and fine-tuning the complete model with all weights unfrozen \raisebox{-0.25em {\includegraphics[scale=0.015]{images/fire.pdf .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: At the inference stage, only binary CCH predictions used for final classification.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=1.03\linewidth]{images/deb_mtl.png} \caption {Two principal component decomposition of PCA for texts from the development subsample. In (a) the vector space structure for the deberta-v3-base fine-tuned in single-task mode is shown, while figure (b) shows the same model but fine-tuned in MTL mode with two additional custom classification heads.} \label{fig:pca}
elements.sent-2411-043: We focused on the monolingual subtask, carrying out comparisons among models and ablations of the best system.
elements.sent-2411-044: For these we employed the original training and development splits provided by the organizers.
elements.sent-2411-045: Our objective here was to reveal the quality improvement in multi-task training compared to single-task training.
elements.figure-2411-003: \begin{tabular}{lcc} Model Development Test TF-IDF with LogReg 63.53 60.93 DeBERTaV3 base 82.56 78.52 MTL: 1 stage 80.51 78.67 MTL: 2 stage 87.33 81.55 MTL: 2 stage + threshold 87.96 83.07 \end{tabular} \caption{Results of model comparison on the test and development set. The highlighted metric is macro $F_1$-score (\%).} \label{tab:comp}
elements.sent-2411-046: As models for comparison we chose Logistic Regression classifier \cite{logreg with TF-IDF features on word n-grams, and DeBERTa-v3 fine-tuned in two-stage mode described earlier, but in single-task setting.}
elements.sent-2411-047: In the MTL approach, we compared checkpoints from different stages, and also explored the effect of adding thresholds on the output of the final classifier.
elements.sent-2411-048: We chose DeBERTa-v3 base for the baseline and the backbone in our system, as it is currently state-of-the-art model for supervised fine-tuning for binary classification~\cite{Macko_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-049: The results are presented in Table \ref{tab:comp .}
elements.sent-2411-050: It can be seen that there is a weak correlation between the gap within the predictions on the dev and test subsamples.
elements.sent-2411-051: For example, the presence of a threshold after the final layer affected the dev result only slightly, but at the same time allowed us to achieve a winning result on the test set.
elements.sent-2411-052: The hyperparameters of the final model are given in Appendix \ref{sec:appendix_b .}
elements.figure-2411-004: \begin{tabular}{rlc} Rank System $F_1$-score (\%) 1 Advacheck (germgr) 83.07 2 tmatchitan 83.01 3 karla 82.80 15 baseline 73.42 36 nitstejasrikar 44.89 \end{tabular} \caption{Final results on the official ranking. Bold denotes our team’s placement.} \label{tab:results}
elements.sent-2411-053: Table \ref{tab:results reports the leaderboard results on the test set, where our system, Advacheck, achieves a macro $F_1$-score of 83.07\%, outperforming approaches of the other participants and ranking first.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Our solution surpassed the claimed baseline by 10\%.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{images/thresholdsv1.png} \caption{Macro $F_1$-score on the test set of different configuration of the systems depending on the threshold.} \label{fig:thresholds}
elements.sent-2411-055: We made a comparison of text embeddings after fine-tuning stages.
elements.sent-2411-056: Samples from development part of data were forwarded to the Transformer-encoder and [CLS] vectors were extracted as outputs.
elements.sent-2411-057: We visualised these vectors using PCA in Figure \ref{fig:pca .}
elements.sent-2411-058: We observe that the alignment of the representations, which was mentioned above, introduces a cluster domain-wise structure.
elements.sent-2411-059: Although the clusters are not perfectly separable, a meaningful difference between the standard BERT-like model and MTL fine-tuning pipelines can be seen.
elements.sent-2411-060: Additional decompositions are presented in Appendix \ref{sec:appendix_e .}
elements.sent-2411-061: To provide further analysis on the multi-task setup we experimented with configurations of our systems, changing the number of multiclass CCH.
elements.sent-2411-062: The system on the leaderboard has 2 Multiclass CCH, and we ran ablation experiments with 1 CCH and 3 CCH.
elements.sent-2411-063: The results are in the Table~\ref{tab:ablations .}
elements.sent-2411-064: Setups with 1 and 3 CCH showed better performance on the development set, but marginally dropped in performance on the test set compared to setup with 2 CCH.
elements.sent-2411-065: What is also interesting is that the results obtained on HC3-trained CCH are similar to the results obtained in M4GT-trained CCH, although M4GT has 10 times more training data than HC3.
elements.sent-2411-066: Additionaly, we experimented with the threshold values on all our configurations.
elements.sent-2411-067: The figures are shown in the Figure~\ref{fig:thresholds and reaffirm the choice of the final system and threshold for it.}
elements.figure-2411-006: \columnwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{lcc} Task Head Development Test HC3 92.27 82.70 M4GT 91.70 81.07 MTL (HC3 + M4GT) 87.96 83.07 HC3 + M4GT + MAGE 91.43 79.23 \end{tabular} } \caption{Comparison of different configurations of heads and tasks trained simultaneously in MTL architecture. The highlighted metric is macro $F_1$-score (\%).} \label{tab:ablations}
elements.sent-2411-069: The two datasets with the highest percentage of incorrect predictions are Mixset~\cite{zhang2024llmasacoauthor and CUDRT~\cite{tao2024cudrtbenchmarkingdetectionmodels , while the texts in the other datasets are detected with very high precision.}}
elements.sent-2411-070: We attribute this to the additional manipulations with these texts, such as rewriting, ``humanizing'' and other editing, done by the authors of the initial datasets.
elements.sent-2411-071: Other texts in the test set and in train set, on the other hand, are the raw output of generation models, without post-processing them, or otherwise not stated.
elements.sent-2411-074: The proportions of correct and incorrect predictions on test set with respect to different generators are shown in Figure~\ref{fig:proportion_generators .}
elements.sent-2411-075: The majority of texts in the test set are either human-written or generated with gpt-4o and our system predicted labels for them very accurately, therefore compensating the poorer performance on other generators.
elements.sent-2411-076: Still, our system is not robust enough yet to the change of generators, as on texts from some of unseen detectors and we dedicate our future works to it.
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{images/simpler_generators.png} \caption{Proportion of predictions for different generators from test set. Labels in bold are generators texts from which are present in the train set.} \label{fig:proportion_generators}
elements.sent-2411-077: In this paper we described the system by the Advacheck team in the monolingual subtask at GenAI Detection Task 1 competition.
elements.sent-2411-078: We proposed solution with multi-task learning architecture that consists of shared Transformer Encoder and composition of one binary and two multiclass Custom Classification Heads.
elements.sent-2411-079: Our system obtained the best results in the official ranking bypassing the baseline by 10\%.
elements.sent-2411-080: Adding tasks for training in parallel reveal the formation of a cluster structure in the space of embeddings, helping to achieve high results despite the presence of a large amount of noisy data.
elements.sent-2411-081: Also, it has been demonstrated that training a similar model but in single-task mode loses to the proposed approach, and configurations with one or three multiclass heads also perform worse than our final system.. .
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=0.36\textwidth]{images/head.png} \caption{The architecture of the custom classification head used in the described approach. Value of dropout is equal to 0.5.} \label{fig:head}
elements.sent-2411-082: In our approach, we replaced the default one-layer linear classifier with a more extended version by adding multiple layers, the final structure of Custom Classification Head (CCH) is shown in Figure \ref{fig:head .}
elements.sent-2411-083: We chose GELU \cite{gelu as the activation feature and added dropout.}
elements.sent-2411-084: In earlier experiments, when compared with the base head, this adaptation gives a higher quality therefore we used it in all subsequent experiments.
elements.figure-2411-009: \begin{tabular}{lcc} Hyperparameters 1st stage 2nd stage Epochs 1 1 Learning rate (LR) 3e-4 3e-6 Warmup steps 50 75 Weight decay 0.01 0.01 Batch size 32 16 Classifier threshold - 0.92 \end{tabular} \caption{Hyperparameters for fine-tuning MTL architecture. We trained for 1 epoch in both stages with possibility of early exit.} \label{tab:hyperparameters}
elements.sent-2411-085: Our final system is MTL architecture with shared deberta-v3-base encoder and 3 CCH.
elements.sent-2411-086: The training of main system was conducted on NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 and the training of other configurations on NVIDIA A100.
elements.sent-2411-088: The organizers of the competition provided data for the train and development stages of the evolving solutions.
elements.sent-2411-089: This is the continuation and improvement of the SemEval Shared Task 8 samples.
elements.sent-2411-090: New domains and generation models were added to the data; details of train and dev sets are shown in Table \ref{fig:data .}
elements.sent-2411-091: In addition, a separate development dataset was available on the CodaBench\footnote{\url{https://www.codabench.org/competitions/3734/ platform where the competition was held; its statistics are shown in Table \ref{fig:data_dev .}}}

elements.sent-2411-093: In addition to the PCA decomposition of text embeddings after passes on our system, we mapped the logit decomposition of two multiclass heads -- CCH on HC3 and CCH on M4GT.
elements.sent-2411-094: We sampled the texts from the dev set, passed them through the encoder and the corresponding classifiers, and then decomposed the logits.
elements.sent-2411-095: From Figure \ref{fig:pca_add , we can observe that the data after the classifiers passes remain in the expected cluster structure intended by shared encoder.}
elements.sent-2411-096: On the dataset HC3 this can be seen more clearly.
